sections.plain_summary: H. pylori (Helicobacter pylori) is a bacterial infection of the stomach lining that affects roughly half the world's population. Many people carry it without obvious symptoms, but in others it causes persistent stomach pain, nausea, bloating, and ulcers. It is a leading cause of peptic ulcers and a recognised risk factor for certain stomach cancers. It is treatable with a specific combination of antibiotics and acid-suppressing medication, though treatment failure is increasingly common due to antibiotic resistance.
sections.detailed_summary:
Helicobacter pylori is a gram-negative, spiral-shaped bacterium that colonises the gastric mucosa — the protective lining of the stomach. It survives the hostile acidic environment by producing urease, an enzyme that neutralises stomach acid in its immediate vicinity. Once established, it triggers chronic inflammation of the stomach lining (chronic gastritis), which over years can progress to peptic ulcers, gastric atrophy, intestinal metaplasia, and in a minority of cases, gastric adenocarcinoma or MALT lymphoma.

Transmission occurs via the oral-oral and fecal-oral routes, most often in childhood and in settings with poor sanitation or household crowding. Infection rates vary dramatically by geography and socioeconomic background — very high in parts of Africa, Asia, and Latin America, and lower but still significant in Western countries.

The majority of infected individuals — estimated at 70–80% — remain asymptomatic throughout their lives. In those who do develop symptoms, the clinical picture is dominated by upper GI complaints: burning or gnawing epigastric pain, postprandial bloating, nausea, and early satiety. Ulcers, when they form, can cause more severe pain and in serious cases may bleed, causing iron-deficiency anaemia or, in emergencies, haematemesis.

Diagnosis can be made non-invasively via urea breath test, stool antigen test, or serology, or invasively via endoscopic biopsy with rapid urease test or histology. The urea breath test and stool antigen test are the most accurate for active infection and for confirming eradication after treatment.

First-line treatment typically involves a 10–14 day course of triple or quadruple therapy — combining one or two antibiotics (commonly clarithromycin, amoxicillin, metronidazole, or tetracycline) with a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) and sometimes bismuth. Eradication rates have declined in many regions due to rising clarithromycin and metronidazole resistance, making antibiotic susceptibility testing increasingly relevant before treatment in some settings.

Even after successful eradication, some individuals continue to experience GI symptoms — a pattern sometimes called post-eradication functional dyspepsia — which may reflect lingering mucosal damage, altered gut microbiome, or pre-existing hypersensitivity.

prevalence.summary: One of the most common bacterial infections in humans — roughly half the global population is infected, though rates vary widely by region.
prevalence.estimate: ~44% globally; up to 80–90% in some low-income countries; 30–40% in Western Europe and North America
prevalence.notes: Prevalence declines with improved sanitation across generations. Most people are infected in childhood. Rates in developed countries have been falling, but significant socioeconomic and ethnic disparities persist. Serology-based estimates may overestimate current active infection because antibodies persist after eradication.

subgroups.name: Asymptomatic carriers
subgroups.description: The majority of infected individuals — estimated 70–80% — carry H. pylori without noticeable symptoms. Infection is often discovered incidentally.
distinguishing_features: No upper GI pain or dyspepsia
May have low-grade chronic gastritis detectable only on biopsy
Still carry long-term risk of ulcer or cancer if infection persists

subgroups.name: Peptic ulcer disease
subgroups.description: H. pylori is the underlying cause in approximately 70–90% of duodenal ulcers and 60–70% of gastric ulcers. Eradication substantially reduces ulcer recurrence.
distinguishing_features: Epigastric burning or gnawing pain, often related to meals
Pain relief with food (duodenal) or worsening with food (gastric)
Possible melena or haematemesis if ulcer bleeds
Confirmed by endoscopy and biopsy

subgroups.name: H. pylori-associated dyspepsia
subgroups.description: A subset of people have upper GI symptoms attributable to H. pylori without macroscopic ulceration — often called H. pylori-positive functional dyspepsia.
distinguishing_features: Epigastric discomfort, nausea, bloating, early fullness
No ulcer on endoscopy
Symptoms may partially or fully resolve after eradication in some individuals

subgroups.name: Antibiotic-resistant strains
subgroups.description: A growing subgroup in whom standard triple therapy fails due to bacterial resistance — most commonly to clarithromycin or metronidazole.
distinguishing_features: Failed first-line eradication course confirmed by post-treatment test
Higher rates in regions with high antibiotic use
Requires culture-guided or alternative regimens (e.g. bismuth quadruple therapy, rifabutin-based regimens)

subgroups.name: Post-eradication persistent symptoms
subgroups.description: Some individuals continue to have upper GI symptoms after confirmed eradication — sometimes described as post-H. pylori functional dyspepsia.
distinguishing_features: Eradication confirmed by urea breath test or stool antigen (≥4 weeks post-treatment)
Ongoing bloating, nausea, or epigastric discomfort
May overlap with functional dyspepsia, SIBO, or altered gastric motility
Requires investigation of alternative drivers rather than repeat antibiotic treatment

subgroups.name: H. pylori and gastric cancer risk
subgroups.description: A minority of infected individuals — particularly those with certain strains (CagA-positive), atrophic gastritis, or a family history of gastric cancer — carry meaningfully elevated long-term cancer risk.
distinguishing_features: CagA-positive strains associated with higher virulence
Atrophic gastritis or intestinal metaplasia found on biopsy
Higher risk in East Asian populations and those with first-degree relatives with gastric cancer
Surveillance endoscopy may be recommended in high-risk individuals

symptoms.notes: Felt in the upper-central abdomen, often described as gnawing or burning. May be worse before meals or at night in duodenal ulcer; may worsen with eating in gastric ulcer.
Particularly common in active infection and around meals.
Feeling full quickly or gassy after small amounts of food — may reflect gastritis-related impaired accommodation of the stomach.
Can occur as a result of functional gastric changes from chronic inflammation.
Often accompanies active infection or ulceration.
More concerning symptom that warrants prompt investigation, particularly to rule out malignancy.
Suggests upper GI bleeding from an ulcer — requires urgent medical attention.
Indicates significant upper GI bleeding — a medical emergency.

May result from chronic occult bleeding from gastric or duodenal ulcers, or from impaired iron absorption due to hypochlorhydria. H. pylori is an underrecognised cause of unexplained iron-deficiency anaemia.
Some evidence links H. pylori colonisation to halitosis, possibly via urease activity and gastric reflux.
The relationship with reflux is complex — H. pylori can both suppress and contribute to acid-related symptoms depending on which part of the stomach is affected.
May be secondary to anaemia, poor nutrient absorption, or the systemic inflammatory burden of chronic infection.
Chronic atrophic gastritis from H. pylori can impair intrinsic factor production and B12 absorption, leading to fatigue, numbness, and cognitive symptoms.

key_patterns: Persistent or recurrent epigastric pain, especially with any alarm features (weight loss, anaemia, dysphagia, vomiting blood)
Unexplained iron-deficiency anaemia, particularly when other causes have been excluded
Peptic ulcer confirmed on endoscopy — H. pylori testing should be routine in this context
Family history of gastric cancer or personal history of gastric atrophy
Failure of standard acid-suppression therapy for dyspepsia without prior H. pylori testing
Post-treatment symptom persistence — confirmation of eradication (not assumed) is essential before pursuing other diagnoses

at_home.name: Symptom tracking
at_home.description: Tracking the timing, nature, and triggers of upper abdominal symptoms (relation to meals, time of day, food types, stress) can provide useful clinical context before testing. A simple food-symptom diary kept for 1–2 weeks may help identify patterns.
at_home.safety_notes: Symptom tracking alone cannot diagnose H. pylori. Alarm symptoms (bleeding, severe pain, significant weight loss) should prompt urgent medical evaluation rather than self-monitoring.

clinical.name: Urea Breath Test (UBT)
clinical.description: The patient swallows a small amount of labelled urea. If H. pylori is present, its urease enzyme breaks down the urea and the labelled carbon is detected in exhaled breath. Highly accurate for active infection and for confirming eradication. Must be performed at least 4 weeks after completing antibiotics and at least 2 weeks off proton pump inhibitors.
clinical.availability: Widely available in hospital gastroenterology departments and many primary care settings. Some private clinics also offer it. Requires advance preparation (stopping PPIs and antibiotics as above).

clinical.name: Stool Antigen Test (SAT)
clinical.description: Detects H. pylori antigens in a stool sample. Accurate for both initial diagnosis and post-treatment confirmation. Less affected by PPI use than urea breath tests but still benefits from stopping PPIs 2 weeks before testing.
clinical.availability: Available through most GPs and gastroenterology clinics. Some private labs offer direct-to-consumer stool antigen testing.

clinical.name: Serology (IgG antibody blood test)
clinical.description: Detects antibodies to H. pylori in blood. Cannot distinguish active from past infection — antibodies persist for months to years after eradication. Useful in populations where prior testing has not occurred, but not reliable for confirming eradication.
clinical.availability: Routinely available via GP blood tests. Often the first test ordered, though its limitations should be understood.

clinical.name: Endoscopy with biopsy (Rapid Urease Test and/or histology)
clinical.description: Upper GI endoscopy allows direct visualisation of the stomach and duodenum. Biopsy samples are tested via rapid urease test (detects urease activity in tissue) and/or sent for histological examination. Provides the most complete picture — can confirm infection, assess mucosal damage, identify atrophy or metaplasia, and rule out malignancy.
clinical.availability: Requires referral to gastroenterology. Typically reserved for those with alarm features, persistent symptoms, or failed non-invasive testing. In some countries, recommended as first-line in individuals over 55 with new dyspepsia.

clinical.name: Culture and antibiotic susceptibility testing
clinical.description: Biopsy samples can be cultured to grow H. pylori and test which antibiotics will be effective. Increasingly recommended before second-line or rescue treatment given rising resistance rates. PCR-based resistance testing from biopsy or stool is also being developed.
clinical.availability: Available in specialist centres; not routine in all hospitals. Increasingly recommended by European and UK gastroenterology guidelines for patients who have failed one eradication attempt.

related_conditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
related_conditions.explanation: H. pylori is found in a significant proportion of people with functional dyspepsia, and eradication resolves symptoms in a subset. After eradication, some people continue to have dyspeptic symptoms that then meet criteria for functional dyspepsia — a well-recognised post-infection pattern.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: Post-infectious IBS can follow acute or chronic H. pylori infection, likely due to mucosal damage, altered gut motility, and disruption of the gut microbiome. H. pylori eradication involving antibiotics may also transiently alter the lower GI microbiome.

related_conditions.name: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO)
related_conditions.explanation: H. pylori infection and the hypochlorhydria it can cause may reduce the stomach's ability to act as a barrier against bacterial colonisation of the small intestine, potentially facilitating SIBO development. Antibiotic treatment for H. pylori may also disrupt the small bowel microbiome.

related_conditions.name: Gut dysbiosis
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic H. pylori infection alters the gastric and intestinal microbiome. Eradication treatment — typically involving two antibiotics — can cause significant short-term disruption to the gut microbiome, sometimes requiring probiotic support during or after therapy.

related_conditions.name: Peptic ulcer disease
related_conditions.explanation: H. pylori is the single most common cause of peptic ulcers (both gastric and duodenal). Chronic gastric inflammation disrupts the mucosal barrier, allowing acid to erode the underlying tissue.

related_conditions.name: Gastric cancer
related_conditions.explanation: H. pylori is classified as a Group 1 carcinogen by the WHO. Chronic infection can progress through a sequence of atrophic gastritis, intestinal metaplasia, dysplasia, and eventually gastric adenocarcinoma in a minority of individuals — particularly with virulent CagA-positive strains.

related_conditions.name: MALT lymphoma
related_conditions.explanation: Low-grade gastric MALT (mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue) lymphoma is strongly associated with H. pylori infection. H. pylori eradication alone causes remission in a significant proportion of early-stage cases.

related_conditions.name: Iron-deficiency anaemia
related_conditions.explanation: H. pylori can cause iron deficiency through occult gastric bleeding, impaired iron absorption due to hypochlorhydria, and possibly direct competition for dietary iron. Unexplained iron-deficiency anaemia that doesn't respond to supplementation should prompt H. pylori testing.

related_conditions.name: Vitamin B12 deficiency
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic atrophic gastritis caused by H. pylori can impair intrinsic factor secretion and reduce gastric acid-dependent B12 release, leading to B12 deficiency and its neurological and haematological consequences.

related_conditions.name: Chronic atrophic gastritis
related_conditions.explanation: Long-standing H. pylori infection can lead to progressive loss of gastric glandular cells (atrophy), reducing acid and intrinsic factor secretion. Atrophic gastritis is a precancerous lesion and an independent risk factor for gastric cancer.

related_conditions.name: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD/GERD)
related_conditions.explanation: The relationship between H. pylori and reflux is complex. H. pylori involving the antrum tends to increase acid secretion, potentially worsening reflux. Conversely, H. pylori involving the corpus may reduce acid output. Some studies suggest eradication modestly worsens GERD in some patients, particularly those with corpus-dominant infection.

related_conditions.name: Idiopathic thrombocytopaenic purpura (ITP)
related_conditions.explanation: H. pylori eradication has been shown to improve platelet counts in a subset of patients with ITP, suggesting an immune cross-reactivity mechanism. H. pylori testing is now recommended in ITP guidelines in many countries.

related_conditions.name: Rosacea
related_conditions.explanation: Several studies have identified higher H. pylori prevalence in rosacea patients, and some report improvement in skin symptoms after eradication — though evidence is not consistent and the relationship remains under investigation.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: H. pylori infection activates mucosal mast cells and promotes local histamine release. In susceptible individuals, this may contribute to or exacerbate mast cell-related symptoms. Some patients with MCAS report improvement in their broader symptom picture following H. pylori eradication.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: H. pylori infection may impair diamine oxidase (DAO) activity in the gastric and intestinal mucosa, reducing histamine breakdown capacity. This could contribute to histamine intolerance symptoms in infected individuals.

strategies.name: H. pylori eradication therapy
strategies.description: The primary treatment for active H. pylori infection is a course of combination therapy — typically 10–14 days of a proton pump inhibitor combined with two antibiotics (commonly clarithromycin + amoxicillin, or metronidazole + amoxicillin). In regions with high clarithromycin resistance, bismuth quadruple therapy (bismuth + PPI + tetracycline + metronidazole) may be used as first-line. Eradication must be confirmed by urea breath test or stool antigen test at least 4 weeks after completing antibiotics.
strategies.notes: Antibiotic resistance is rising globally. If first-line treatment fails, culture-guided second-line therapy is increasingly recommended rather than empirical retreatment. Full adherence to the course is essential — partial completion markedly increases resistance risk.

strategies.name: Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) for symptom relief
strategies.description: PPIs (e.g. omeprazole, lansoprazole) reduce gastric acid secretion and can provide significant symptom relief for epigastric pain and heartburn while eradication treatment is under way or when ulcer healing is needed. They are a component of eradication regimens.
strategies.notes: PPIs are often continued for 4–8 weeks after antibiotic completion to support ulcer healing. Long-term PPI use carries potential risks (B12 and magnesium depletion, altered gut microbiome, bone density effects) and should not be continued indefinitely without indication.

strategies.name: Bismuth subsalicylate
strategies.description: Bismuth has direct antibacterial activity against H. pylori and forms a protective coating on ulcerated mucosa. It is a key component of bismuth quadruple therapy and may also provide symptomatic relief for nausea and gastric discomfort.
strategies.notes: Should be used as part of a prescribed protocol, not as a standalone treatment for H. pylori.

strategies.name: Probiotics as adjunct to eradication
strategies.description: Several probiotic strains — particularly Lactobacillus reuteri, Saccharomyces boulardii, and multi-strain formulations — have been studied as adjuncts to eradication therapy. They may modestly improve eradication rates, reduce antibiotic-associated side effects (especially diarrhoea and nausea), and support gut microbiome recovery.
strategies.notes: Timing matters — probiotics are generally taken during and immediately after the antibiotic course, not before. Should be taken at a different time of day from antibiotics where possible. Cannot replace antibiotics for eradication.

strategies.name: Mastic gum
strategies.description: A resin from the Pistacia lentiscus tree, mastic gum has demonstrated antibacterial activity against H. pylori in laboratory studies and some small clinical trials. Some individuals use it alongside conventional treatment or in cases where antibiotics are not immediately accessible.
strategies.notes: Clinical trial evidence in humans is limited and mixed. It is not a replacement for antibiotic-based eradication, but may be of interest to those researching adjunct or complementary approaches.

strategies.name: Broccoli sprout / sulforaphane
strategies.description: Sulforaphane, found in high concentrations in broccoli sprouts, has demonstrated anti-H. pylori activity in several small human trials, including reduction of urease activity and antigen positivity. Some integrative practitioners use it as an adjunct or in post-eradication support.
strategies.notes: Evidence is promising but preliminary. Trials have generally been small. Not a substitute for antibiotic eradication in active symptomatic infection or ulcer disease.

strategies.name: Dietary modification for symptom comfort
strategies.description: While no specific diet cures H. pylori infection, certain adjustments can reduce symptom burden during and after treatment: eating smaller, more frequent meals; avoiding spicy, fatty, or acidic foods; reducing caffeine and alcohol; not eating close to bedtime. These changes reduce acid burden on an inflamed or ulcerated mucosa.
strategies.notes: Dietary change addresses symptoms, not the infection itself. Food restrictions should be relaxed once eradication is confirmed and mucosal healing has occurred, unless another underlying condition persists.

strategies.name: Iron and B12 supplementation
strategies.description: For those with confirmed iron-deficiency anaemia or B12 deficiency secondary to H. pylori, supplementation is appropriate alongside eradication therapy. In some cases, restoring normal gastric acid and intrinsic factor production after eradication is sufficient to allow natural recovery of absorption.
strategies.notes: Levels should be rechecked after eradication and recovery to assess whether ongoing supplementation is needed.

things_to_avoid: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, aspirin, naproxen) — greatly increase the risk of ulcer formation and ulcer bleeding in H. pylori-infected individuals; avoid entirely during active infection and ulcer healing
Alcohol during active infection and eradication treatment — irritates the gastric mucosa, may interact with metronidazole causing severe nausea
Smoking — impairs mucosal healing, increases ulcer recurrence risk, and may reduce eradication rates
Stopping the antibiotic course early — incomplete courses dramatically increase the risk of treatment failure and antibiotic resistance
Assuming eradication without testing — symptoms improving does not confirm H. pylori has been eliminated; confirmation requires a breath or stool test at least 4 weeks post-treatment
Long-term PPI use without review — often continued beyond what is clinically necessary; carries its own risks including micronutrient depletion and microbiome disruption
Caffeine and acidic drinks during active gastritis or ulcer — can worsen mucosal irritation and pain

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Testing 'positive' on a blood antibody test does not mean someone currently has H. pylori — antibodies persist for months to years after the infection has cleared. A significant number of people are treated unnecessarily or re-treated when the original infection was already gone.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Doctors often order serology because it is easy and cheap. Patients receive a positive result and assume it means active infection. Questioning the result feels like questioning the doctor.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Insisting on a urea breath test or stool antigen test — both of which detect active infection — before starting antibiotic treatment avoids unnecessary treatment and its side effects. If only serology was done, it is reasonable to ask for confirmation with an active infection test.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: H. pylori eradication does not always resolve all GI symptoms. A meaningful proportion of people continue to experience bloating, nausea, or epigastric discomfort after confirmed eradication — because H. pylori was not the only driver, or because the microbiome and mucosa need time to recover.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: After enduring a difficult antibiotic course and waiting weeks for confirmation, patients expect to feel completely better. Ongoing symptoms feel like treatment failure or a sign the bacteria is still present, leading to demands for re-testing and re-treatment.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Once eradication is confirmed, persistent symptoms should be investigated for other causes — post-infectious functional dyspepsia, SIBO, altered gastric motility, or microbiome dysbiosis. Repeat antibiotic courses targeting H. pylori when eradication has already been confirmed is not appropriate and harms the microbiome.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: First-line eradication therapy fails in 20–40% of cases in many Western countries due to antibiotic resistance, and most clinicians do not test for resistance before prescribing. Without susceptibility testing, second courses may use antibiotics that will not work.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Resistance testing requires endoscopy and culture, which feels disproportionate for what seems like a simple infection. Patients and many clinicians assume the standard course will work.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: If a first eradication attempt fails, requesting antibiotic susceptibility testing (via endoscopic biopsy and culture, or PCR-based methods) before undertaking a second regimen leads to meaningfully better outcomes than empirical retreatment.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: H. pylori infection acquired in childhood, left untreated for decades, can cause irreversible changes to the stomach lining (atrophy, metaplasia) that persist even after eradication and continue to carry cancer risk.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Eradication feels like a complete cure. People reasonably assume that clearing the infection removes the cancer risk entirely and that no further monitoring is needed.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People diagnosed with atrophic gastritis or intestinal metaplasia on biopsy should discuss endoscopic surveillance intervals with a gastroenterologist even after successful eradication — guidelines recommend periodic monitoring in this group.

key_levers.lever: Confirm active infection before treating, and confirm eradication after treating
key_levers.why_it_matters: Treating without confirmation leads to unnecessary antibiotic exposure and microbiome damage. Assuming eradication without testing means ongoing infection may go undetected while symptoms are attributed elsewhere.
key_levers.how_to_start: If not already done, request a urea breath test or stool antigen test rather than relying on serology. After completing treatment, book a follow-up breath or stool test 4–6 weeks later.
key_levers.timeline: Testing takes days; results guide all subsequent decisions.

key_levers.lever: Complete the full prescribed eradication course without gaps
key_levers.why_it_matters: H. pylori eradication regimens are specifically designed as timed courses. Incomplete adherence — even missing a few doses — allows surviving bacteria to develop antibiotic resistance, making future treatment harder.
key_levers.how_to_start: Use a pill organiser and set phone reminders for each dose. If side effects are severe, contact the prescribing clinician to discuss antiemetics or dose timing adjustments rather than stopping the course.

key_levers.lever: Request culture-guided therapy after first-line failure
key_levers.why_it_matters: Empirically prescribing a second antibiotic regimen without knowing the resistance profile is likely to fail again, increasing cumulative antibiotic exposure and microbiome damage.
key_levers.how_to_start: If a urea breath test confirms ongoing infection after a full treatment course, ask the treating clinician specifically about endoscopic biopsy for culture and sensitivity testing before starting another regimen.
key_levers.timeline: Biopsy results typically available within 2–4 weeks; treatment can follow.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Iron, B12, and sometimes vitamin D deficiencies can develop insidiously over years of infection-related gastric damage. These deficiencies can cause fatigue, cognitive symptoms, and neurological effects that persist even after H. pylori is cleared unless specifically addressed.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a blood panel including ferritin, serum iron, full blood count, B12, and folate. Discuss results with a GP or functional medicine practitioner, and recheck 3–6 months after eradication.
key_levers.timeline: Iron stores take several months to replenish; B12 may take longer to show neurological improvement.

key_levers.lever: Support microbiome recovery after antibiotic treatment
key_levers.why_it_matters: The two-antibiotic eradication regimens cause significant disruption to gut microbiome diversity, which can contribute to ongoing GI symptoms. Active support during and after treatment may reduce collateral damage.
key_levers.how_to_start: Take a clinically studied probiotic (e.g. Saccharomyces boulardii, Lactobacillus reuteri) during and for 4–8 weeks after the antibiotic course. Prioritise dietary fibre and fermented foods in the recovery period.
key_levers.timeline: Microbiome diversity typically begins recovering within weeks; full recovery may take months.

common_mistakes: Relying on serology alone for diagnosis or to confirm eradication — antibody tests cannot detect active vs. past infection
Stopping antibiotics early due to side effects without consulting a clinician, causing treatment failure and promoting resistance
Assuming symptoms have resolved because the antibiotic course is finished — confirmation test is always required
Repeating identical eradication regimens after first-line failure without resistance testing — likely to fail again for the same reason
Continuing long-term PPIs indefinitely after eradication without reassessing whether they are still needed
Ignoring persistent post-eradication symptoms or attributing them automatically to H. pylori relapse — other conditions frequently co-exist or are unmasked after treatment
Not testing and treating close household members or sexual partners when reinfection is suspected — H. pylori can be transmitted within households

specialists.role: The primary specialist for H. pylori — manages endoscopic investigation, biopsy, ulcer diagnosis, cancer surveillance, and second-line or rescue eradication therapy after failed first-line treatment.
specialists.notes: Most essential for: alarm symptoms, failed eradication, confirmed atrophic gastritis or metaplasia, or any concern about gastric malignancy. Referral thresholds vary — some GPs manage straightforward cases without specialist referral.

specialists.role: First point of contact for most H. pylori presentations. Can order non-invasive tests (urea breath test, stool antigen, serology), prescribe first-line eradication therapy, and manage straightforward post-treatment follow-up.
specialists.notes: GPs vary in familiarity with current treatment guidelines, resistance rates, and the importance of confirming eradication. Some may rely on outdated protocols or not routinely test for eradication after treatment.

specialists.type: Functional medicine doctor or integrative gastroenterologist
specialists.role: May be helpful for those with persistent symptoms after confirmed eradication, seeking to investigate microbiome disruption, nutritional deficiencies, or co-existing conditions (SIBO, dysbiosis, functional dyspepsia) that are not routinely investigated in conventional care.
specialists.notes: Variable evidence base for some integrative approaches. Look for practitioners who combine conventional diagnostics with adjunct investigation rather than those who avoid standard testing.

specialists.role: Useful in the post-eradication recovery phase, particularly for rebuilding a disrupted gut microbiome, managing ongoing GI symptoms, or addressing nutritional deficiencies (iron, B12) through diet.
specialists.notes: Most relevant if symptoms persist after eradication and there is a need to investigate dietary drivers — a FODMAP-trained dietitian may be helpful if post-eradication symptoms resemble IBS or dyspepsia.

specialists.role: May be involved if H. pylori is identified as a driver of unexplained iron-deficiency anaemia that has failed to respond to supplementation, or in the context of H. pylori-associated ITP (immune thrombocytopenic purpura).
specialists.notes: Collaboration between gastroenterology and haematology is sometimes needed in these cases.

patient_communities.name: r/HPylori
patient_communities.notes: Large, active community of people going through H. pylori diagnosis, treatment, and recovery. Useful for understanding treatment experiences, side effect management, and navigating the system. Peer support rather than medical advice — quality varies but many threads contain well-researched information.

patient_communities.name: H. Pylori Warriors & Fighters Support Group
patient_communities.notes: Facebook group with a large membership sharing experiences of diagnosis, treatment protocols, side effects, and post-eradication recovery. Particularly useful for those who have failed first-line treatment.

patient_communities.name: Gastro Patient Forum – Helicobacter pylori
patient_communities.notes: UK-based patient forum with threads covering diagnosis, treatment experiences, and symptom persistence. Useful for people in the UK navigating NHS pathways.

learning_resources.title: Helicobacter pylori – Patient Information
learning_resources.description: Clear, evidence-based patient-facing overview of H. pylori — how it is acquired, what it causes, how it is diagnosed and treated, and what to expect after treatment. A good starting point for people newly diagnosed.

learning_resources.title: Management of Helicobacter pylori infection — The Maastricht VI/Florence Consensus Report
learning_resources.author_or_source: European Helicobacter and Microbiota Study Group
learning_resources.description: The most authoritative international guideline on H. pylori management, covering diagnosis, treatment regimens including resistance-guided therapy, special populations, and surveillance. Detailed and technical but publicly accessible — useful for people who want to understand what best-practice care looks like.

learning_resources.title: H. Pylori: Everything You Need to Know
learning_resources.description: Accessible video overview covering H. pylori biology, symptoms, testing approaches, and treatment — including discussion of natural adjuncts and post-treatment gut recovery. Useful for people who prefer visual learning and want a more integrative perspective alongside conventional medicine.

learning_resources.title: The Gut Health Doctor — H. pylori article series
learning_resources.author_or_source: Dr. Megan Rossi / The Gut Health Doctor
learning_resources.description: Evidence-based gut health content including articles on H. pylori, microbiome recovery after antibiotics, and dietary support. Well-suited to people looking for practical dietary and lifestyle guidance grounded in science.

learning_resources.title: Helicobacter pylori and iron deficiency anaemia: guilty as charged?
learning_resources.description: Reviews the evidence linking H. pylori to iron-deficiency anaemia — a connection many clinicians overlook. Useful for people whose anaemia has not responded to supplementation and who want to understand potential gastric causes.

learning_resources.title: Probiotics in the treatment of Helicobacter pylori infection: A comprehensive review
learning_resources.description: Systematic review of evidence for probiotics as adjuncts to H. pylori eradication — covering which strains have been studied, their effects on eradication rates and side effects, and practical implications.

sections.relevance_signal: **Upper gut symptoms** — nausea, burping, upper abdominal discomfort after eating. Worse with NSAIDs. May have no symptoms at all but contributes to low stomach acid.
